beginner.question: What is the basic structure of an HTML document?
beginner.answer: An HTML document has four main parts: <!DOCTYPE html>, <html> as the root element, <head> for metadata, and <body> for visible content.

beginner.question: What is the purpose of the <head> section?
beginner.answer: The <head> section contains metadata about the page including title, character encoding, viewport settings, stylesheet links, and meta tags for SEO.

beginner.question: What is the role of the <body> tag?
beginner.answer: The <body> tag contains all the visible content of the web page including text, images, links, tables, and other elements that users see.

beginner.question: What does <!DOCTYPE html> do?
beginner.answer: It declares that the document is written in HTML5 and tells the browser to render the page in standards mode rather than quirks mode.

beginner.question: What is a meta tag?
beginner.answer: A meta tag provides metadata about the HTML document. Meta tags are placed inside <head> and are not visible on the page but affect SEO, social sharing, and browser behavior.

beginner.question: What is the <title> tag used for?
beginner.answer: The <title> tag defines the page title that appears in the browser tab and is used by search engines as the clickable headline in search results.

beginner.question: What happens if you forget the DOCTYPE?
beginner.answer: The browser switches to 'quirks mode,' which causes inconsistent rendering and layout bugs because the browser tries to emulate old, non-standard behavior.

beginner.question: Can you put content directly inside the <html> tag?
beginner.answer: The <html> tag should only contain <head> and <body> as children. Content should go inside <body>.

beginner.question: What is the lang attribute used for?
beginner.answer: The lang attribute on the <html> tag declares the language of the page (e.g., en for English). It helps screen readers pronounce text correctly and assists search engines.

beginner.question: What is the difference between <head> and <body>?
beginner.answer: <head> contains metadata (title, links, scripts) that is not displayed. <body> contains all visible page content that users interact with.

intermediate.question: What is the purpose of Open Graph meta tags?
intermediate.answer: Open Graph (OG) meta tags control how web pages appear when shared on social media platforms like Facebook, Twitter, LinkedIn, showing a title, description, and image.

intermediate.question: What is the viewport meta tag and why is it important?
intermediate.answer: The viewport meta tag (<meta name='viewport' content='width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0'>) ensures web pages render correctly on mobile devices by controlling the layout viewport.

intermediate.question: How does the browser build the DOM tree?
intermediate.answer: The browser parses the HTML byte stream, tokenizes it into tags, builds a node tree, and constructs the DOM. Each HTML element becomes a node in the tree.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between <section> and <article>?
intermediate.answer: <section> groups related content thematically. <article> represents a self-contained, independent piece of content that could be reused or syndicated (like a blog post).

intermediate.question: Why should <meta charset> be the first element in <head>?
intermediate.answer: It ensures the browser knows the character encoding before parsing any other content, preventing misinterpretation of special characters.

intermediate.question: What is the maximum recommended length for a meta description?
intermediate.answer: The meta description should be 150-160 characters. Longer descriptions may be truncated in search engine results.

intermediate.question: Can you have multiple <header> elements on a page?
intermediate.answer: Yes, you can have multiple <header> elements. Each section or article can have its own header, but there is typically one main page header.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between <main> and <div>?
intermediate.answer: <main> is a semantic element representing the dominant content of the page. Only one <main> is allowed per page. <div> is a generic container with no semantic meaning.

intermediate.question: What is the purpose of a favicon?
intermediate.answer: A favicon is a small icon displayed in the browser tab, bookmarks, and search results. It helps users identify your site visually.

intermediate.question: What does the <link> element do in the <head> section?
intermediate.answer: The <link> element establishes relationships between the current document and external resources, most commonly CSS stylesheets and favicons.

advanced.question: What is the critical rendering path and how does HTML structure affect it?
advanced.answer: The critical rendering path is the sequence of steps the browser takes to render a page: DOM construction, CSSOM construction, render tree, layout, and paint. HTML document structure, especially the order of <script> and <link> elements, directly impacts rendering performance.

advanced.question: How does browser quirks mode differ from standards mode?
advanced.answer: Quirks mode emulates old browser behaviors (like IE5/6 box model) for backwards compatibility. Standards mode follows W3C specifications strictly. The DOCTYPE determines which mode is used.

advanced.question: What is the Shadow DOM and how does it relate to the main document DOM?
advanced.answer: Shadow DOM is a browser technology that encapsulates a DOM subtree within a web component, isolating it from the main document. It has its own DOM tree that does not interfere with the main document's DOM.

advanced.question: How does the HTML parser handle script tags with async and defer attributes?
advanced.answer: Without attributes, the parser blocks on <script> tags. 'async' downloads in parallel and executes as soon as available. 'defer' downloads in parallel but executes in order after parsing completes. Both improve perceived performance.

advanced.question: What is the purpose of the <template> element in HTML?
advanced.answer: The <template> element holds HTML fragments that are not rendered immediately. Its content is parsed but inactive until cloned into the live DOM via JavaScript, enabling efficient client-side templating.

advanced.question: How do microdata and structured data relate to HTML document structure?
advanced.answer: Microdata attributes (itemscope, itemtype, itemprop) add semantic meaning within HTML elements, marking up content for search engines. They are embedded within the existing HTML structure and help generate rich snippets.

advanced.question: What is the difference between the DOM and the HTML source?
advanced.answer: The HTML source is the raw document sent by the server. The DOM is the live, in-memory representation constructed and potentially modified by the browser. JavaScript can manipulate the DOM but not the source.

advanced.question: How does the <head> section affect Largest Contentful Paint (LCP)?
advanced.answer: Resources linked in <head> (fonts, preloads, preconnects) affect LCP by controlling how quickly critical resources are discovered and loaded. Preloading hero images and critical fonts in <head> can improve LCP scores.

advanced.question: What is the purpose of the <base> element in HTML?
advanced.answer: The <base> element sets the base URL for all relative URLs in the document. It can also set the default target attribute for all links. Only one <base> element is allowed per document.

advanced.question: How does document structure affect accessibility for screen reader users?
advanced.answer: Proper document structure with semantic elements (headings hierarchy, landmarks like <nav>, <main>, <aside>) allows screen reader users to navigate quickly between sections. Correct heading nesting (<h1>-<h6>) creates a logical document outline.
